title: Incursa.Quic Public API Surface Verification
verification_method: Execution, inspection, and API-diff evidence from the public API analyzer, plus targeted surface tests derived from the Microsoft public-surface behavior baseline and the existing connection/runtime seams.
preconditions: The approved SPEC-QUIC-API and ARC-QUIC-API-0001 artifacts exist.
The Incursa.Quic project builds with public API analyzer support enabled.
The consumer facade types are available for inspection or promotion.
The Microsoft reference tests used as behavioral evidence have been reviewed and mapped to the refined surface expectations.
scope: This verification slice covers the connection-, listener-, and first client-entry consumer public API surface, the close/dispose/error projection visible through that surface, the server-side listener validation and accept semantics, the supported positive client/server loopback establishment boundary, the pending client-connect validation/cancellation/terminal semantics, and the internal-only boundary. It proves that only the approved facade types are public, that `QuicStreamType` has the direction-only shape, that `QuicListener` and `QuicListenerOptions` honor validation, cancellation, and disposal, that `QuicClientConnectionOptions` and `QuicConnection.ConnectAsync(...)` expose only the approved shape, that `ConnectAsync(...)` and `AcceptConnectionAsync(...)` return real `QuicConnection` facades only after the supported establishment boundary is reached, and that the listed helper/runtime seam types remain internal. It does not attempt to prove stream entry points, full TLS-auth parity, wire behavior, packet parsing beyond the bootstrap path, or performance.
expected_result: The public API matches the approved connection-, listener-, and first client-entry consumer surface, the close/dispose/error projection is visible, `QuicStreamType` has the approved direction-only shape, the listener validation and accept semantics are honest, the supported positive loopback establishment boundary is real, pending client connect is honest about cancellation and unsupported TLS settings, and the helper/runtime seam types remain internal.
procedure: Compare src/Incursa.Quic/PublicAPI.Unshipped.txt and any future API approval diffs against the approved type list.
Run compile-time or reflection checks that the approved public types and members are present.
Run negative access checks that the helper and runtime seam types cannot be referenced from outside the assembly.

Add or review focused consumer-behavior tests for `QuicStreamType` shape, close/dispose/error mapping, read-side terminal behavior, listener validation, accept cancellation/disposal, positive client/server loopback establishment, narrow callback invocation, client option/connect validation, pending-connect cancellation/disposal, unsupported TLS rejection, and facade-to-runtime/host mapping.
Review the design note, spec, and the reference Microsoft test evidence together to confirm no extra public abstractions were introduced.
status_summary: Execution evidence now covers the supported positive client/server loopback boundary, the listener/server slice, and the pending terminal honesty for connect and accept. The proof target is client option/connect validation, unsupported TLS rejection, and terminal cancellation/disposal behavior; full TLS-auth and stream entry points remain out of scope.

supplemental_sections.heading: Evidence Notes
supplemental_sections.content: The current proof includes build output and focused requirement-home tests for `REQ-QUIC-API-0001`, `REQ-QUIC-API-0002`, `REQ-QUIC-API-0005`, and `REQ-QUIC-API-0008`, including the supported positive loopback establishment tests. The next evidence addendum for this artifact is expected to cover the stream-entry slice and broader TLS/auth parity while the connection-boundary proof remains stable.

supplemental_sections.heading: Reference Behavioral Evidence
supplemental_sections.content:
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\QuicListenerTests.cs`
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\QuicConnectionTests.cs`
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\QuicStreamTests.cs`
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\QuicStreamConnectedStreamConformanceTests.cs`
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\MsQuicCipherSuitesPolicyTests.cs`
- `C:\src\dotnet\runtime\src\libraries\System.Net.Quic\tests\FunctionalTests\MsQuicTests.cs`

These tests are not target-repo proof, but they define the baseline public-behavior expectations that the target verification plan should mirror.
